meta.title: Mercedes C320 C350 CDI Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI engine — OM642 V6 diesel. W203 and W204 generations, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI Engine Era Map
Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM642 engine typically costs £2,500–£6,000 supply only depending on generation and variant, with the C350 CDI sitting at £3,500–£8,500. Fitting adds £900–£1,500. Oil cooler seal replacement alone costs ~£1,000 at an independent specialist. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 is rated ★★½☆ to ★★★☆☆ depending on generation. It's a smooth, powerful engine, but it has well‑documented issues: oil cooler seals (the most common), timing chain stretch, turbo actuator failure, PCV valve failure, and EGR clogging. The engine is reliable when well‑maintained but has a high labour cost for common repairs. 2008–2013 examples are considered the worst years.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil cooler seal leaks (cheap parts, expensive labour), timing chain stretch (cold‑start rattle), turbo actuator failure (loss of power), PCV valve failure (~100,000 miles), EGR valve clogging, swirl flap failure, and crankshaft seizure on some 2008–2013 examples.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well‑kept W204 C350 CDI worth £4,000–£10,000, a £4,500 recon is a significant investment — assess carefully. For a W203 C320 CDI worth £2,000–£5,000, the economics are marginal. However, well‑maintained examples with documented oil cooler seal and timing chain work are worth saving.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The C320 CDI uses OM642.910 (W203, 2005–2007) or OM642.960 (W204, 2007–2010). The C350 CDI (2010–2014) uses OM642.960. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM642's timing chain, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A timing chain and tensioner replacement (£1,500–£3,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£2,500–£8,500 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, book an inspection immediately. The chain tensioner is a known weak point — replacing it with updated parts is strongly recommended.

eraMap.title: The C320 CDI / C350 CDI THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: First-generation OM642 — 224 bhp; oil cooler seals, swirl flaps and timing chain are the main watchpoints.
Early W204 OM642 — oil cooler seals, turbo actuator, PCV and EGR issues are common.
The same 3.0 V6 as the C320, renamed C350 CDI from 2010 — 224 bhp, 510 Nm.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz C320 / C350 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C320 / C350 CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM642's V‑layout makes many repairs labour‑intensive — the oil cooler seal replacement alone costs ~£1,000 at an independent specialist.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz C320 / C350 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour‑intensive but straightforward; catch it before oil loss causes damage
If caught early; tensioner and chain replacement prevents catastrophic failure
Refurbish or replace actuator; avoid full turbo replacement
Replace PCV valve; common around 100,000 miles
Regular cleaning; use quality fuel and motorway runs to prevent
Labour‑intensive but repairable; common on early OM642s
Some 2008–2013 engines suffer crankshaft seizure due to low oil feed

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C320 / C350 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with documented oil changes (using MB 229.52 spec oil)
* Evidence of oil cooler seal replacement — this is the number one preventative item
* Post‑2013 build (the later OM642s had many issues resolved)
* No AdBlue system (earlier cars without AdBlue are generally more reliable)
avoidIf: * Cold‑start rattle with no timing chain history — this is the single most important check
* Oil leak from the bottom of the engine — potential oil cooler seal failure
* Loss of power — potential turbo actuator failure
* 2008–2013 build without documented oil change history — crankshaft seizure risk
* Poor service history — the OM642 is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C320 CDI / C350 CDI's OM642 V6 diesel is a smooth, powerful engine that offers excellent performance and economy. But it has well‑documented weaknesses: oil cooler seals (cheap parts, expensive labour), timing chain stretch, turbo actuator failure, and PCV/EGR issues. Buy on service history and proof of preventative maintenance — the OM642 can be a reliable engine when properly cared for, but neglected examples can become money pits.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz C320 / C350 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking noise on startup (timing chain warning — the most critical check)
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM642.910 for W203 2005–2007, OM642.960 for W204 2007–2014) — see the Era Map above
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car or oil dripping from the bottom of the engine (oil cooler seal failure)
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any loss of power or whistling? Turbo actuator failure risk
* Idle Quality — Any rough idle or hesitation? EGR or PCV valve issues
* Oil Pressure — Any low oil pressure warnings? Crankshaft seizure risk on some 2008–2013 examples
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, boost, or emissions issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures; often point to EGR or DPF problems

items.label: Most Common Failure
items.value: Oil cooler seal leaks — approximately 55% of OM642 enquiries

items.label: Peak Enquiry Season
items.value: Winter — cold starts expose timing chain issues; oil leaks worsen in cold weather
